title: Conflict: New Engineering Team Leader
text:
Situation: A highly experienced, 8-year veteran developer in our company was promoted to lead my Scrum team. During his transition to management, he naturally leaned into his comfort zone—coding. He took on a large amount of complex tasks, which created a bottleneck and affected our sprint pacing.


Task: My goal was to support him in this transition and get our sprint delivery back on track, all while maintaining a strong, collaborative PM-R&D relationship.


Action: * Data-driven observation: I noticed through our daily stand-ups and sprint data that his workload was unbalanced and certain tasks were delayed.


  • Collaborative 1-on-1: I set up a private, casual sync. Instead of discussing missed deadlines, I framed the conversation around workload management and his new role. I validated his technical expertise, but gently highlighted how empowering his team through delegation would free him up to guide the overall architecture.


Result: He was very receptive. Within a few days, he began delegating effectively. Our sprint burndown chart stabilized, and we improved our sprint completion rate from roughly [60%] to [90%], hitting our deadlines successfully.


Learning: I learned that as a PM, empathy is just as important as analytics. Recognizing when a colleague is going through a professional transition and offering constructive, private support is key to building a high-performing team.

title: Failure / Entrepreneurial Story: "Easy Monkey" Startup
text:
Situation: * I co-founded a startup called "Easy Monkey".

It was a gamified app where users played games to win local restaurant discounts.

Our business model: We only made money when users actually went to the restaurant to redeem the coupon.


Task: * My job was to build the product, get users to play, and drive real-world foot traffic to the businesses (local restaurants and coffee bars)


Action (The Mistake): * We made a classic mistake: we built a "perfect" app from day one instead of a quick Lean MVP.


Our marketing was actually great. Thousands played the games and won coupons.

But here was the problem: nobody went to the restaurants to redeem the coupon.

Instead of stopping to talk to users, we just built more features. We added countdown timers and aggressive push notifications to force them to go. It didn't work.


Result: * We had huge engagement in the app, but our actual revenue was practically zero.

After about a year, we ran out of money and had to pivot into a marketing agency just to survive.


Learning (The Takeaways): 

* This was an expensive but amazing lesson for me as a PM.

First, I learned the danger of "Vanity Metrics". We got excited about game engagement, instead of focusing on our true North Star metric—which was actual coupon redemptions.

Second, I learned to never build from 0 to 100 again. Today, I always test the riskiest assumption first with a simple MVP, before polishing any code.

title: 🏆 Success: Large-Scale Refactoring & Phased A/B Testing
text:
Situation: * Our core product was a video player used on hundreds of publisher websites, getting tens of millions of views every day.


  • We had to completely rebuild it with modern technology.
  • The biggest risk? We couldn't afford to break the product or hurt our clients' revenue while doing it.

Task: * I led the product strategy and the exact rollout plan for this high-risk migration.

Action (The Strategy): * Instead of doing one massive update, the engineering team and I broke the player down into separate layers (like Infrastructure, Layout, and Monetization).


  • We rebuilt and released it layer by layer.
  • To play it safe, I used a strict A/B testing strategy. We released the first new layer to just 10% of global traffic.
  • Because of our huge scale, that 10% gave us millions of data points to verify that revenue and stability were perfectly fine. Once validated, we ramped it up to 100%, and moved to the next layer.

Result (The Impact): * After a few months, we successfully migrated the entire player.


  • The best part? We had absolutely 0% negative impact on client monetization during the transition.


Even better, the new architecture was a huge upgrade: we improved load times by 15%, reduced crashes by 10%, and cut our integration time by almost 50%.

title: 🏆 Success: The "Build vs. Buy" SEO Win
text:
Situation: * At Minute Media, our sports network had 350 million monthly viewers. To keep users on the site longer, we integrated Taboola. It generated quick revenue, but it created a massive problem: it slowed down our page loads and crashed our Google Search ranking. We lost our #1 spot.

Task: * I needed to fix the performance and SEO drop. The biggest challenge? I had to convince management to kill a tool that was actively making money.

Action (The Strategy): * I pitched building our own in-house "Related Articles" widget using our internal data. I aligned with engineering and made a data-backed case to management: regaining our SEO traffic was worth more than Taboola's short-term revenue. Instead of a massive project, we built a Lean MVP in just one sprint. We ran a head-to-head A/B test: our in-house widget vs. Taboola.

Result (The Impact): * The test proved our in-house widget loaded faster AND got more clicks. We completely removed Taboola. The metrics: Session time improved by ~10%, pages per session jumped by ~40%, and most importantly, we successfully regained our #1 position on Google Search.

title: 🏆 Success: The Missing API & Data-Driven Pushback
text:
Situation: * At Primis—a video discovery and monetization platform for publishers—I managed a scrum team handling integrations and zero-to-one projects. I noticed a gap in our product based on my past experience: we were missing a key API feature that all our competitors had.

Task: * I wanted to build it, but management pushed back hard. They rejected it due to strict time constraints and other priorities. I needed to prove the business value.

Action (The Data & Execution): * During a company QBR, the Sales team presented a slide showing that 15% of lost deals were directly caused by this missing API. I literally took a picture of that slide! I brought that exact data to my next management meeting to build my business case, and got the green light. To minimize risk, we built a Lean MVP in just one sprint. We then partnered with our Content team to work with a design partner and to get real-world feedback, iterated quickly, and fully deployed it.

Result (The Impact): * * The feature was shipped fast and successfully.

  • During that exact same period, Sales managed to close a massive 2 million dollars deal with an enterprise client specifically because we finally had this API.
  • The bigger picture: It actually unlocked a whole new tier of larger enterprise clients for the company. These were huge deals we simply couldn't compete for without this feature.
